speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Ready to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you've come to Sulzfeld.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came to Sulzfeld. There, they arranged that it should be a day-- day-- daytime, you know, shift and the nighttime shift. But one week, we worked at-- at night time, and one week we worked at daytime. We came there. They gave me tool. I worked in the factory where I was filling in, in the shells, the little bullets we-- I used to make. Each machine was-- the machine was so big, like a half of this room. And we used to-- we got the-- they oiled those shells in a bucket, a wooden bucket. The bucket itself weighed probably about 25 pounds. Was a wooden, big bucket. And then you had to fill it in until the break, until the end, had to fill it in with-- with those shells. Each time I picked up that bucket and I throw it into the machine, I thought mine-- the world is ending. And if-- if one shell didn't go into the right place, then you-- you saw right away fire out from the machine. It was very scary. And I said, well, here we're going to end. This is going to be the end for us because working so hard with no food, it's impossible. Every day, we all, you know, deteriorating. Our health was deteriorating every day, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we were young. And we said to each other, what can we do? We'll see. If we'll survive, it's going to be our luck. If not, it's not. What can we do? We left-- we-- we-- they took us away from our parents, took us away from the sisters and brothers. We have nobody. I was by myself. And I didn't know, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never-- I was always under, you know, protection from my parents. And here, I was alone. I had to manage myself whatever I could. I did the best I could in a decent-- decent way. And we worked at that-- in that-- that factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans themselves, some of them, pitied us, seeing us, such small children, not developed even. Skinny. How can they work that kind of work? They gave us the work what they had to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were walking around and managing with us, you know, telling us, do this, do that, you know. And we had to work for them. The Germans were all day with us in the factory. Even if you have to go out to the ladies' room, you couldn't go by myself. You went with a woman, German woman. She went with us, waiting for us outside until we're through, and then brought us back into the factory. That's how we worked probably about two years, from 1944-- no, one year until 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, some days the only day was one day off, on Sunday. So we didn't know what to do before. We start doing something for ourselves. We had to take care of ourselves, wash some clothes and be clean, you know, to go next day to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the morning, every Sunday, the Germans came in. He says, who wants to go to work voluntarily? So I was always the one. I raised my-- my hand and I says, I'm going. I went to work. Why? Because I wanted to get at least something to eat. So we went. They took us to work to private people. Private citizens, the German citizens. If somebody had to unload a big truck of vegetables or potatoes or coals, for the winter for them, and put everything in-- in a-- a basement. They should be prepared for the winter. So I went to work there. And some others girls with me, too. And we worked practically until 4:00, 5 o'clock afternoon. The people pitied us. They gave us something home to the camp. Slice of bread, few potatoes, or some carrots or other vegetables.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came in-- so we had for the next day. Only one week-- one day in the week, they gave us wood to heat up the oven because wintertime, we were freezing there. We didn't have nothing to, you know, to cover up ourselves. We slept two girls on one bunker, two ups-- on the upper bunker and two on the lower bunker. We were, you know, just huddling to each other to be warm. And Sunday was the lucky day for us. They gave us some wood. We heated up the room. And you were sitting around if you didn't go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you went to work, it was different. But a day when we didn't go to work, we were sitting around the fire, put some potatoes in the fire and sit around and-- and tell our stories. What kind of stories could we tell? From home.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did you know better than from home, because they took us from home. We-- each was telling to the next its stories. And we were singing. Singing-- sitting around, there was singing. At least a little bit humor we had to show, because otherwise without a little bit humor, you know, you are no human being at all. So-- and this was the days when we didn't work. On the days when we worked, it was terrible. You couldn't go out. We were walking around, let's say, in the camp, not when you work. When we were there and we were not working. But usually, we were working during the day. So we came home in the evening, and we had to go right to bed to get to sleep. And we were-- when we worked night shift, so we came 5 o'clock in the morning, so we had to go to sleep too, and be ready for the next hour to go back to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this way, we didn't have much chance even to go out and to catch some fresh air. But working, I was working with-- with my four girls, with-- the one who slept with me and the two who that slept above me on the bunker. So we were, you know, always together, and each one-- each one of us was thinking about the next one, you know. We were all in the same shoes. And we try, you know, to be very friendly with each other and help each other as much as we could because this was the only way. Otherwise, you wouldn't be able even to survive. Each of us, each-- each gave the next one a little bit support, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking maybe there's going to be a day when we're going to be free. So it was working. It was like this for the last-- like I said, a year there in that camp. I didn't know too many people in this camp because we didn't have any very chance. It was the only chance was when we could see somebody is during the day when we worked in the same-- you know, in the same factory. But unfortunate, not all of us worked in the same one, because they had different departments, you know. Other factories, they did-- they used to do other-- other, you know, kind of work. I made those-- those little bullets for the shotguns. Other people made different bullets, you know, bigger ones, maybe for the-- bombs for the airplanes. This was going on. We were working and working and working, in this was going one like this for-- until the liberation. Until we start feeling something in camp, that something is fishy there. You used to-- they used to-- used to cut off the light, the lights in the-- in the-- in the barracks, you know, in the bungalows. That they shouldn't see that we have light, they shouldn't know, because the Americans at that time knew every little place where they had the prisoners there. And they were flying around and circling around, you know, in camp, but they didn't bomb us because they knew that were camps. They had all the informations there. In the same city, in Sulzfeld, there was next, not far from us, not-- I mean, not far from the factory where we worked, was there a camp from French prisoners. But they were not only-- they were free. Those prisoners were free. They could go out from camp. They got packages from their parents, from-- from friends. They send them in packages with food. But we didn't know that. But when we start feeling that the-- the air was changing, you know. We didn't have light so often. We didn't have light every night in camp. So we didn't know what's going on. Nobody knew because we didn't go out. We didn't know what's going on outside of the camp. We were in a different world, you know, just selected from everyone and-- and, you know, isolated from everybody. And around the-- we had fences around, electrical fences, you know. Couldn't go-- you couldn't get, you know, close to the fence because you-- you were afraid you were going to be electrocuted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, we noticed some-- some of the guys, you know, were walking around, they were around the fence, and they told us they couldn't talk to us because they were not allowed to talk to us. Two guys came around the fence, and they said to us, don't you-- didn't you notice that the light is not anymore every night on like it used to be before? We said, what do we know what's going on outside the world? We were like the sheep-- like the sheeps there in the-- in the camp. They said, you be-- you just watch out, watch and be outside-- more outside the barracks, that you should get some information. And you know then-- then you will know what's going on outside the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, we noticed that we didn't get any food. It was quite a few days. They didn't cook. Nothing was there. The Germans start, you know, running away from the camp, the one who was with us inside. Because knew what's going-- they had the papers. They had the news from radio. But we didn't have nothing. And they start running away because they were afraid that the American will come in, they'll be in big trouble. So one day, I said-- we had the girls from our camp. They were working in the kitchen. But nobody was allowed besides them. Nobody was allowed to go into the kitchen. And we were hoping that one day if the kitchen will be open, we'll go all run into the kitchen and take everything out, whatever we-- we'd be able to do. So it happened not so fast, but it was going on quite some times, probably practically a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, we got up in the morning. We went in there. The kitchen is open. The doors are wide open. So we said, what's going on here? We went in-- walked into the kitchen, something unusually-- this would never happen. Nothing there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No people cooking. What they cooked-- what they cooked for us is in the morning, we got a slice of bread with a coffee. In daytime, we had the soup. And the soup was-- was cooked without tomatoes. You-- when you got the soup, you-- you could put in a little net and-- and-- and just search for a piece of potato, you know, like you're catching fish in the water. You had to catch, you know-- search for a piece of potato there. It was only water. And we fill up, all of us, from that, because we had only water, no food, no nourishment, no nothing. So we walked into the kitchen and we see no-- nothing is there. Nobody's there in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say, what's going on? We didn't know what's-- what's happening. Then we walked out from the kitchen. We saw people around the fence, who were the French guys. Two of them were two friends. And they said to us, you-- you watch. You be out-- every day out, because the Americans are getting close.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can imagine how we were happy. And we heard the airplanes coming every night and searching around, searching around, looking around the camps where we're located and taking pictures. And we were happy. We would run out from the-- from the-- from the bunkers, you know, and we were dancing outside in-- in-- in the camp. Oh, I say, now it's coming the day for us. Happy. We were happy, that time. Hungry. No shoes. No clothes. Happy. Because we knew that the Americans are coming close. Then took a few days after that. They were-- they were getting ready, you know. The Germans were getting ready from camp. Slowly, one and by one, one by one, disappeared from the camp. They didn't want to make it for us, you know, that we should notice that something is going on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were disappearing every day. The lady who used to go with us every day to work, it was a-- a lady soldier with a shotgun and a man soldier with a shotgun. You couldn't go by ourselves. You had to go always, you know, with-- be, you know, with-- with the Germans around. So we saw-- we noticed that that German lady, the one used to go with us to work, she was not anymore around there. What's happening? We didn't know. To make short-- the story short, they disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the soldiers, the French soldiers, said to us, you know what? The Americans are very close. Get up in the-- early in the morning and be outside. We got up in the morning, early.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still dark. It was very early. Probably 5 o'clock in the morning. Every-- the whole camp, 1,000 women, climbed on the fence. It was no light anymore, so we could climb on the fence. All of us, we climbed on the fence. The fence was very high. We climbed on the fence because we wanted to see the first soldier, American soldier, come into the camp. The first motorcycle, the first soldier came. He stopped by the fence, by the gate. [SIGHS] Just a second, please. I'm sorry. He stopped and all of us start screaming and crying. We didn't know that this is already our liberation. Came-- the first guy came, and then he was waiting, you know, for the next to come, the next motorcycles with the Jeeps. They came. He-- he stopped there. And they came. They opened up the gate. Another thing what I forgot to mention this, two nights before, we had an old German. He was the commander from the camp. And he-- they told him, the Germans, that they gonna pour over our bungalows gasoline. And as we were asleep at night, they wanted to burn us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- the commander said, I don't agree, and I'm not gonna let them do it. It was two days before the American marched into Sulzfeld to that camp. He said no. He didn't sign the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, not-- not when I'm alive here. Because I kept the girls here alive. Whatever went on in-- in-- inside the camp, I'm not responsible because I am only the one who protect the camp from the outside, that nobody came-- should come in and kill us there. We worked enough for them. And he didn't want us to be killed. So he said, no. And he signed the paper that that he doesn't agree with them. He is not going to allow this to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Americans came in and open up the fence, and we were all of us like a bunch of sheeps, one fell on the next one, out from the camp. So in-- and the Americas came in. He says, what about this guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Says-- we told him not to do nothing to him. He saved our life. Otherwise, he would be-- we wouldn't be here anymore. They took him, took off his belt, took off his-- his gun. And they took him with them. Where they took him, we don't know. But we open up-- they open up the gates. We run out from the camp. The Americans said, now, all of you, behind us. Walk in-- walk with us. They drove, you know, on the motorcycles and on the Jeeps. And they took some girls on the Jeeps, whoever. The rest who couldn't-- who didn't go with them, we walked behind them. They took us into the camp where they were stationed the-- all the American soldiers. And they said to us, we know you're hungry. You thirsty-- you're thirsty. You have no clothes. We had only one uniform. We have to wash them at night, in the morning, put them on. Even sometimes I put my uniform on when it was stiff from when it was winter. It was stiff from the-- from the-- you know, from the cold-- from cold. It was freezing. And I put it on on me because I didn't have any other one to-- to put on, so I had to go to work. And I'd put it on. It was wet. And-- when-- when I put that it on, it was wet on me, and that's how I went to work. The soldiers took us there to their station and they put on the tables out chocolate and food and everything they had there. Unfortunate, we sat down by the table. Everybody was crying because we couldn't eat. Our stomachs were like this, you know. Very small. They shrank. And we were afraid to eat. I said to the girls, don't eat because we're going to get sick, and we're going to die. We didn't die during the war. We're gonna die after.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't do that. We-- we used our senses. You know? And they said to us, take a little bit at a time. Just a little piece of bread, something, you know, small.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went back-- back went-- back to the camp where we were. But-- but the gates were open. We were free that time. We could go in and out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was going on some quite a few weeks until, you know, they-- they got control everything, the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to myself, like that, I'm not gonna stay here on this bloody earth. I want to leave from here as fast as I can. But where should I go? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met, by coincidence, I met a friend of mine from my city town, my town home. And he came. He said, look. Next from your camp is another camp. I said, I want to go and look for my brother, for my sister. I says, I know a camp where there are no-- no men, only women. If you want to go with me, I'll take you. If you're not gonna find anybody, I'll bring you back to the camp. One day, he said, I'm gonna come. I'm gonna get you, and you're gonna go and search for your brother, for your sister. He came. He picked me up. And I went with him to the next camp. And I checked over there. Every little bungalow there, every place I could, but I didn't find nobody. My sister was not there. Unfortunate, my sister remained in Germany at the time. And I was also in Germany, but in a different city. Then I met my present husband. He was there in that city. It was Hillesheim in Germany. He came also with a whole bunch of-- of his friends. They came into the-- to that city. They went into a building where the mayor of the city lived. And he said to them, we would like you to leave this house. We have no place where to sleep. We have no place where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all the time sleeping on your bed. Let us sleep here once in a bed, feel like a-- like a human being. We would like you to leave this house. He says, what's the matter? I'm not gonna leave this house. This is my house.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yeah. After, you can come back to this house. But now, right now, let us be in this house. He occupied the building. It were probably 40-- 40 rooms there. And in the building, in the basement, you had everything your heart desired. Food and meat and salamis and drinks and-- and-- and you know, and-- I mean, whiskeys and everything. Name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I met my husband, that time-- he was not my husband. He just-- my friend who brought me to this camp introduced me to him. And he says, you know what? I have a building now where you can take-- pick some-- you know, choose a girlfriend or whoever, two girls. And he gave us a room. So I was lucky. The room was very little. He gave me the room for myself. So I lived there quite for few weeks. And then my friend came. He said to me, if you would like, we're gonna-- mine husband is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, it's-- it makes a group of people. He's gonna have a group of people going to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you would be interested to go to Israel, then I will put you on the list. And this was-- this happened. I says, well, I didn't find my brother. Didn't find my sister. And I don't know where to go to look for them. But I figured that if somebody's gonna be alive, I'll find out later. And I registered to go to Israel. But we didn't know that we not going to Israel straight ahead. So he had-- he had a group of thousand people, boys and girls, everybody who was liberated from camp. I registered myself with him. One day, we came from the city to Markkleeberg. And over there, we went on the planes-- on the trains. And they shipped us, all of us, you know. We went by train. We didn't know where we're going, actually. We thought we're gonna go straight to Israel, but we knew that we cannot go by train to Israel. We have to go by ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: But maybe they going to take us to a place where it's a port city. They're gonna take us to a-- to the ship. But no. They brought us over to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Belgium with-- with the train. We came down Belgium, the whole group, a thousand people. Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a-- a building from the Jewish Federation. They had a building, five-story building. And that building had before students, foreign students, lived in this building. And during the war, it was also war there in-- in Belgium because they were-- they were not occupied from the Germans, but the-- the Germans were quite a bit-- you know, they spent some time there, too. And they took a lot of people from there, too, also to-- to the camps. So they-- so he-- they told-- they gave us this building, and they put in each room two, three girls or two-- two, three boys in the same-- I mean, in each different room. We were there. And all the Jewish people from that city-- it was Charleroi next to Brussels-- came to visit us because they didn't-- they-- they thought probably who knows how we look. We looked very bad. It was not-- short after the war. And some people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here. We have to change--
